content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901              323-6441              322-6444

March 2, 1984


Michael Evers
Assemblyman John Vasconcellos
Room 6062, State Capitol
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                        Re: Our File No. A-84-034

Dear Mr. Evers:

     This is to confirm my advice to you of February 22, 1994,
regarding the following questions:

     1. Is a legislator prohibited from sending a legislative
        newsletter or other mass mailing at public expense to
        persons outside of the district for which he has filed
        a declaration of candidacy?

     2. Is a legislator prohibited from making two or more
        mailings to persons located in the district for which
        he has filed a declaration of candidacy if each mailing
        is sent to fewer than 200 persons in the district, and
        each mailing is sent to persons representing a par-
        ticular industry group regarding pending matters which
        will affect that particular industry group?

     Government Code Section 89001 prohibits an elected state
officer from sending at public expense any legislative newsletter
or other mass mailing after he or she has filed a declaration of
candidacy. By regulation, the Commission has determined that
such mailings are not prohibited so long as less than 200 pieces
of mail are sent in a calendar month to persons in the district
for which the officer has filed a declaration of candidacy.
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18901--copy enclosed.) Therefore,
the prohibition contained in Government Code Section 89001 would
not apply to mailings which are sent to persons outside of the
official's declared district.

     With respect to your second question, Government Code
Section 82041.5 defines the term "mass mailing" to mean:

     ... two hundred or more identical or nearly
     identical pieces of mail, but does not

Michael Evers
March 2, 1984
Page 2


                    include a form letter or other mail which is
                    sent in response to a request, letter or other
                    inquiry.

     Regardless of the industry group affiliation of the recipients,
so long as the mailings are not "identical or nearly identical"
and each mailing is sent to fewer than 200 persons in the district
for which the official has filed a declaration of candidacy, the
prohibition in Government Code Section 89001 would not apply.
Generally, mailings which discuss the same subject(s) are con-
sidered to be "identical or nearly identical" regardless of
their format. If you would like more specific guidelines,
however, examples of proposed mailings must be provided.

     Please let me know if you have additional questions.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [illegible]
                                        Carla J. Wardlow
                                        Political Reform Consultant


Enc.

sections.question:
1. Is a legislator prohibited from sending a legislative newsletter or other mass mailing at public expense to persons outside of the district for which he has filed a declaration of candidacy?

2. Is a legislator prohibited from making two or more mailings to persons located in the district for which he has filed a declaration of candidacy if each mailing is sent to fewer than 200 persons in the district, and each mailing is sent to persons representing a particular industry group regarding pending matters which will affect that particular industry group?
sections.conclusion: Government Code Section 89001 prohibits an elected state officer from sending at public expense any legislative newsletter or other mass mailing after he or she has filed a declaration of candidacy. By regulation, the Commission has determined that such mailings are not prohibited so long as less than 200 pieces of mail are sent in a calendar month to persons in the district for which the officer has filed a declaration of candidacy. Therefore, the prohibition contained in Government Code Section 89001 would not apply to mailings which are sent to persons outside of the official's declared district. With respect to the second question, so long as the mailings are not "identical or nearly identical" and each mailing is sent to fewer than 200 persons in the district for which the official has filed a declaration of candidacy, the prohibition in Government Code Section 89001 would not apply.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks whether a legislator is prohibited from sending legislative newsletters or mass mailings at public expense to persons outside their declared district, and whether multiple targeted mailings to fewer than 200 persons per mailing within the district to specific industry groups are permissible under campaign finance laws.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mailings sent to persons outside the declared district are not prohibited. Multiple mailings sent to fewer than 200 persons per mailing within the declared district are permissible if they are not identical or nearly identical in content, even if targeted to specific industry groups, provided they do not constitute mass mailings under the regulatory definition.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. File number A-84-034 corresponds to document ID 84034. Letter dated March 2, 1984, confirming prior oral advice from February 22, 1984. Signed by Carla J. Wardlow, Political Reform Consultant.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is a legislator prohibited from sending a legislative newsletter or other mass mailing at public expense to persons outside of the district for which he has filed a declaration of candidacy?

2. Is a legislator prohibited from making two or more mailings to persons located in the district for which he has filed a declaration of candidacy if each mailing is sent to fewer than 200 persons in the district, and each mailing is sent to persons representing a particular industry group regarding pending matters which will affect that particular industry group?

CONCLUSION: Government Code Section 89001 prohibits an elected state officer from sending at public expense any legislative newsletter or other mass mailing after he or she has filed a declaration of candidacy. By regulation, the Commission has determined that such mailings are not prohibited so long as less than 200 pieces of mail are sent in a calendar month to persons in the district for which the officer has filed a declaration of candidacy. Therefore, the prohibition contained in Government Code Section 89001 would not apply to mailings which are sent to persons outside of the official's declared district. With respect to the second question, so long as the mailings are not "identical or nearly identical" and each mailing is sent to fewer than 200 persons in the district for which the official has filed a declaration of candidacy, the prohibition in Government Code Section 89001 would not apply.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to questions about whether a legislator may send legislative newsletters and mass mailings at public expense to persons outside their declared district and whether multiple targeted mailings to fewer than 200 recipients within the district are permissible under campaign finance laws.
